text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — POST-TREATMENT RESPONSE ASSESSMENT VISIT

PATIENT: Margaret Chen
ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncologist]
VISIT TYPE: Post-treatment response assessment, approximately 12 weeks post-completion of definitive chemoradiotherapy

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Post-treatment response assessment following completion of definitive concurrent cis-IMRT for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Patient presents for review of restaging PET-CT and MRI neck obtained this week.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old Taiwanese-American female, never-smoker, with a history of HPV-positive (p16+) poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, staged at T2N1M0 per AJCC 8th edition (stage III). Her presenting symptoms were a 3-month history of left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia, and a palpable left neck mass. She underwent workup including MRI neck, PET-CT, and direct laryngoscopy with biopsy confirming the diagnosis. NGS (comprehensive genomic profiling) was notable for PIK3CA p.E545K and TRAF3 loss-of-function mutation; TMB was low (5.2 mut/Mb); MSI-stable.

She was treated with definitive IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions over 7 weeks, simultaneous integrated boost technique) and concurrent weekly cis (40 mg/m2 x 7 planned cycles). She received 6 of 7 planned Platinol cycles — one cycle was held during week 6 for a transient creatinine elevation to 1.2 (from baseline 0.7) and administered in week 7 instead, for a cumulative Platinol dose of approximately 390 mg/m2. Treatment was completed approximately 12 weeks ago.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis and grade 3 dysphagia, for which a prophylactic PEG tube (placed prior to initiation of treatment) was utilized — she became fully PEG-dependent during weeks 5-7 and for several weeks post-treatment. She was hospitalized briefly approximately 2 weeks post-treatment for dehydration and pain management, with uneventful recovery. At her 6-week post-treatment visit, mucositis had largely resolved (grade 1), opioids had been successfully weaned, and she was resuming soft foods — roughly 40-50% of caloric needs orally at that time, with PEG supplementing the remainder.

Today she presents having reviewed her restaging imaging results with the team. She reports feeling considerably better than she did at her last visit. She is eating soft foods and thick liquids with increasing comfort and feels her energy level is substantially recovered. Throat pain is minimal — she is using acetaminophen occasionally but has not needed opioids for several weeks. She has noticed improvement in the ability to swallow, though liquids of thinner consistency remain somewhat challenging. The left neck lymph node she had noticed initially is no longer palpable. She denies any new neck pain, jaw pain, ear pain referred to the left ear, hemoptysis, or voice change. No new lumps or bumps. She is managing her PEG tube without difficulty at home and continues supplemental nocturnal tube feeds. She reports her energy level at approximately 70-75% of her pre-illness baseline. She has begun returning to work part-time (she is a high school principal) over the past 2 weeks.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Xerostomia (persistent, grade 2 — managing with artificial saliva spray and frequent water sips; notices it most prominently when eating dry foods and speaking for extended periods, which impacts her professional role). Dysgeusia (altered taste, grade 1-2 — food tastes "flat" and slightly metallic; some improvement compared to peak treatment period). Mild fatigue (grade 1, improved from treatment period). Supplemental PEG tube feeds ongoing (nocturnal, approximately 30-40% of daily caloric needs).
Negative: No fever, chills, or sweats. No hemoptysis. No significant otalgia. No trismus symptoms subjectively reported (jaw opening feels close to normal to patient). No dyspnea. No new neck masses. No neurological deficits. No chest pain. No bowel or urinary complaints.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing female in no distress. Alert and oriented x3. Affect appropriate.
Vitals: BP 118/74, HR 68, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 58.5 kg (up from 57.0 kg at end of treatment; down approximately 5.6% from pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg).
Head/Face: No facial asymmetry. Mild radiation-related hyperpigmentation over the bilateral neck consistent with prior RT field; no moist desquamation; no skin breakdown.
Oral cavity/Oropharynx: Mucosa with diffuse erythema and mild atrophy consistent with post-radiation changes; no ulcerations, pseudomembranes, or discrete mucosal lesions. Tongue protrusion and lateral mobility intact. Base of tongue evaluated on indirect visualization — no obvious mass or irregularity palpated or seen. Saliva appears sparse (consistent with radiation-induced xerostomia). No trismus on exam — mouth opening approximately 38 mm.
Neck: No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Left level II region — previously palpable nodal mass is now completely non-palpable, with only subtle soft tissue fullness in the region consistent with post-treatment fibrosis. No carotid tenderness. No bruit appreciated on cursory auscultation today.
Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic): Nasopharynx: Normal. Oropharynx: Left base of tongue shows post-radiation mucosal changes — mild thickening, pallor, and atrophic appearance; no discrete mass, ulceration, nodularity, or exophytic lesion identified. Right base of tongue and right lateral wall: Normal. Hypopharynx: Symmetric piriform sinuses, no mass. Epiglottis: Normal appearance, no pooling. Larynx: Bilateral vocal fold mobility normal and symmetric; no supraglottic mass.
PEG site: Clean and well-healed; no surrounding erythema or drainage.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact on screening exam.
Cardiovascular/Pulmonary: Heart RRR. Lungs CTA bilaterally.

---

IMAGING AND LAB REVIEW:

PET-CT (FDG PET/CT, skull base to mid-thigh), obtained approximately 12 weeks post-treatment:
Primary site (left base of tongue): No FDG-avid disease; mild background uptake in the region (SUVmax ~2.1) consistent with inflammatory/post-treatment changes, not suspicious for viable tumor. Left level IIA lymph node: Markedly decreased in size (previously 25 mm, now 8 mm) — no significant FDG avidity (SUVmax 1.8), consistent with fibrotic nodal response rather than residual/recurrent disease. No contralateral or bilateral nodal involvement. No new cervical, mediastinal, or distant FDG-avid disease. Lungs: Clear. Liver: No focal lesions. Bones: No suspicious lesions. Impression: Complete metabolic response (CMR) to chemoradiotherapy.

MRI Neck with contrast (post-treatment protocol), obtained approximately 12 weeks post-treatment:
Left base of tongue: Post-treatment changes; no residual discrete mass or abnormal focal enhancement to suggest persistent primary tumor; mild mucosal thickening consistent with radiation effect. Left level IIA node: Markedly decreased — now 8 mm (previously 25 mm on pre-treatment MRI); no internal necrosis; no pathologic enhancement; consistent with complete nodal response. No new cervical lymphadenopathy. Pharyngeal/laryngeal anatomy intact; no evidence of early radiation necrosis. Carotid arteries patent bilaterally. Impression: Complete radiographic response; no evidence of residual or recurrent disease.

Recent labs (from today's blood draw):
CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 12.4 g/dL, Plt 198 x10^9/L — all within normal limits.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Creatinine 0.71 mg/dL (back to baseline), BUN 14, sodium 139, potassium 4.0, magnesium 1.9 mEq/L (low normal, continue oral supplementation).
TSH: 3.6 mIU/L (patient on pre-existing levothyroxine 50 mcg/day for prior well-controlled hypothyroidism; this value is reassuring; monitoring to continue given risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism developing in coming months to years).
Albumin: 3.5 g/dL (low-normal, trending up from treatment nadir; ongoing nutritional support appropriate).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

52-year-old never-smoking female with HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (stage III, AJCC 8th ed.), s/p definitive concurrent Platinol + IMRT (70 Gy / 35 fractions; 6 of 7 planned weekly Platinol cycles completed), presenting approximately 12 weeks post-treatment for response assessment.

1. ONCOLOGIC STATUS — COMPLETE RESPONSE / NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE:
Both PET-CT and MRI neck obtained at the 12-week post-treatment timepoint demonstrate complete metabolic and radiographic response. There is no FDG-avid or morphologically suspicious disease at the primary site or in the regional lymph nodes. The residual 8 mm left level IIA soft tissue density is non-avid and consistent with fibrosis/treatment effect. This is an outstanding result, consistent with the well-established excellent prognosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma in never-smokers treated with definitive chemoradiotherapy.

I had a detailed discussion with Ms. Chen regarding these imaging findings. She is understandably relieved and emotional. We reviewed what "complete response" means in this context, and I was careful to explain that while these results are very encouraging, ongoing surveillance remains important. She understands and is committed to follow-up.

2. SURVEILLANCE PLAN:
Given the confirmed complete response, we will proceed with standard post-treatment surveillance for HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma:
- Clinical examination and flexible nasopharyngoscopy: Every 1-3 months for year 1 (next visit in approximately 6 weeks, then every 2 months through month 12).
- At 12 months post-treatment: Repeat MRI neck with contrast (annual locoregional surveillance).
- Chest CT: Planned at 18 months post-treatment for secondary primary screening.
- Annual thereafter per institutional surveillance protocol.
- HPV-positive OPSCC patients with complete response at 12-16 weeks post-CRT have very low rates of distant failure; ongoing locoregional surveillance is the priority for this patient.

3. PEG TUBE — PLAN FOR REMOVAL:
Oral intake is currently estimated at approximately 60-70% of caloric needs. She is tolerating soft foods and thick liquids reasonably well. Modified barium swallow is being scheduled through speech/swallowing therapy. Per our standard practice, the PEG will remain in place until she achieves and sustains approximately 80-90% of her caloric needs orally with stable weight, which is anticipated over the next 4-8 weeks. She has been appropriately managing tube feeds at home. We will reassess at next visit.

4. LATE TOXICITY MANAGEMENT:
- Xerostomia (grade 2): Prescribed pilocarpine 5 mg PO TID for salivary stimulation. Reviewed oral hygiene, fluoride trays, frequent hydration. Referral to dentistry for fluoride protocol.
- Dysgeusia (grade 1-2): Counseled that taste recovery typically continues over 6-12 months post-RT, though complete return to baseline is not always achieved. No pharmacologic intervention at this time.
- Dysphagia: Continuing outpatient speech/swallowing therapy with exercises (Mendelsohn maneuver, effortful swallow, tongue base exercises). MBS scheduled.
- Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — monitoring: TSH currently 3.6 mIU/L on levothyroxine 50 mcg/day (pre-existing hypothyroidism). Given radiation to the neck, she is at significant risk of developing or worsening hypothyroidism over the coming months to years. She has been counseled to report fatigue, weight gain, constipation, or cold intolerance. TSH to be rechecked in 3 months.
- Ototoxicity (grade 1 bilateral high-frequency shift): Stable per prior audiogram. Recheck audiology at 12 months. No hearing aids needed at present.
- Magnesium supplementation: Oral magnesium oxide 400 mg BID given persistent low-normal Mg.
- Nutritional status: Albumin 3.5, weight trending up — continuing tube feeds and oral advancement. Nutrition follow-up as needed.

5. MEDICATIONS AT THIS VISIT:
- Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism)
- Pilocarpine 5 mg PO TID (newly prescribed for xerostomia)
- Magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID (for post-treatment hypomagnesemia)
- Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN pain
- Artificial saliva spray PRN
- PEG tube feeds: Jevity 1.2 or equivalent at 60 mL/hr x 8 hours nightly (supplemental)
- Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (for GERD, pre-existing)
- Fluoride trays per dentistry protocol

6. QUALITY OF LIFE / FUNCTIONAL STATUS:
Ms. Chen is returning to work part-time as a high school principal, which reflects meaningful functional recovery. She reports the xerostomia is her main ongoing complaint affecting her professional function (speaking for extended periods). She is managing well psychologically — she was referred to the survivorship clinic for additional support and has been connected with a head and neck cancer peer support resource. ECOG PS 1 today. I anticipate continued functional improvement.

7. OTHER:
- Patient educated on signs and symptoms of recurrence (new neck mass, throat pain, otalgia, hemoptysis) — instructed to call or present urgently if any of these develop between visits.
- HPV vaccination discussion: As a parent, she was counseled on the importance of ensuring her adolescent children have completed the HPV vaccine series.
- Return visit: Approximately 6-8 weeks. Sooner if any clinical concerns.

Time spent in this visit: approximately 45 minutes, including imaging review, examination, counseling, and care coordination. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

— [Attending Physician Signature, Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
